transcript: Welcome to part 4, the final part of lesson 3.1. So far, we have covered the fundamentals of inbound marketing, content marketing, and SEO. Now we are going to tackle the third and final pillar of inbound marketing, social media marketing. This is where you take your valuable content and bring it to the platforms where your audience is already spending hours every day. By the end of this part, you will understand what social media marketing really is, how to choose the right platforms, how to create engaging content, and most importantly, how to integrate social media with your marketing automation systems. This is the final piece of the inbound marketing puzzle. Let's dive in. So what is social media marketing? Social media marketing is using social platforms to attract, engage, and build relationships with your target audience. Notice I said relationships. This is not just broadcasting your message. Social media is called social for a reason. It's about conversation and community. Here's the key difference between social media and other marketing channels. It is two-way. You share valuable content and your audience engages in response. You build relationships. This back and forth creates trust and connection. The social media promise is this. If I show up consistently, provide value, and engage authentically, I will build a community of people who know, like, and trust me. And when those people are ready to buy, I will be the obvious choice. Think of social media as the place where you humanize your brand. Start conversations and pull people into your ecosystem. Once they engage with you on social media, you can guide them to your website, your email list, your lead magnets, and eventually into your sales funnel. Let me give you four compelling statistics that show why social media is essential. Statistic number one, 4.9 billion people use social media worldwide. That is more than half the global population. Your audience is definitely on social media. Statistic number two. The average person spends two and a half hours per day on social platforms. That is a massive amount of attention you can tap into if you show up strategically. Statistic number three. 31% of all website referral traffic comes from social media. If you are not using social media, you are missing out on almost a third of potential website visitors. Statistic number four. 76% of consumers have purchased something they discovered on social media. Social is not just for brand awareness. It directly drives sales. The bottom line is this. Your audience is already scrolling through social media. They are discovering brands, engaging with content, and making buying decisions based on what they see. You just need to meet them where they are. Let me show you how social media works across every stage of the inbound marketing funnel. At that track stage, you use social media to get discovered. You share valuable content, use hashtags strategically, and engage in trending topics. Examples include educational posts, how-to videos, and valuable tips. At the convert stage, you drive traffic from social media to landing pages and lead magnets. You use links in bio, swipe up stories in Instagram, and call-to-action posts that guide people to opt-in. At the close stage, you share social proof, testimonials, and success stories on social media. Customer wins, case studies, and limited-time offers help move people toward purchase. At the delight stage, you provide ongoing tips and celebrate customer success on social media. You share tips for customers, feature their wins, and provide exclusive content. At the advocate stage, you make it easy for happy customers to share and refer. Share buttons, user-generated content campaigns, and referral programs all happen on social media. Social media works at every stage of the funnel, not just awareness. This is what makes it so powerful. Let me break down the five major social media platforms so you understand where to focus your efforts. Platform number one, Facebook. It has 3 billion users and skews toward people 35 years old and above. It is best for community building, groups, paid ads, and local businesses. Content types include posts, videos, stories, live streaming, and groups. It works for both B2B and B2C, but it is stronger for B2C. Platform number two, Instagram. It has 2 billion users with the strongest presence among 18 to 34 years old. It is best for visual brands, lifestyle content, and influencer marketing. Content types include photos, reels, stories, carousels, and IGTV. It is primarily B2C with some B2B applications. Platform number three, LinkedIn. It has 900 million users who are professionals and decision makers. It is the go-to platform for B2B, thought leadership, professional networking, and recruiting. Content types include articles, posts, videos, and company pages. It is primarily B2B. Platform number four, Twitter, also known as X. It has 550 million users across diverse demographics. It is best for real-time updates, news, thought leadership, and customer service. Content types include tweets, threads, spaces, and direct messaging. It works for both B2B and B2C. Platform number five, TikTok. It has 1 billion users, primarily Gen Z and young millennials. It is exploding in popularity for short video content, viral trends, creative storytelling, and brand awareness. Content types include short videos, trends, challenges, and live streaming. It is primarily B2C, but B2B is emerging. The key takeaway. Don't try to be on all platforms. Master one to two where your audience actually spends time. So how do you choose the right platform? Follow your audience, not trends. If your target audience is B2B professionals, decision makers, or corporate clients, go with LinkedIn. That is where they research, network, and make business decisions. If your target audience is young consumers aged 18 to 34 who care about visual products or lifestyle brands, focus on Instagram or TikTok. That is where young audiences discover and engage. If your target audience is older consumers 35 plus who are community-focused, stick with Facebook. It has strong community features and an established user base in that demographic. If your audience cares about news, real-time updates, and trending topics, Twitter or X is the right choice. If your audience is video creative and entertainment TikTok offers explosive organic reach The common mistake I see beginners make is trying to be everywhere They post on Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, Twitter, and TikTok, and they do all of them poorly. It is better to be excellent on one platform than mediocre on five. Choose where your audience is and master that platform first. Now let's talk about what to post. The golden rule is 80-20. 80% of your content should be value-driven. That means education, entertainment, inspiration, problem-solving, behind-the-scenes, and user-generated content. You are providing value without asking for anything in return. Only 20% of your content should be promotional. That is product launches, sales offers, calls to action to buy, and limited time deals. Why this ratio? Because people do not follow you to see ads. They follow you for value. If all you do is promote your products and services, people will tune out and unfollow. But if you provide value 80% of the time, you earn attention and trust. Then, when you do ask for something in that 20%, people are much more willing to listen because you have already given so much. The principle is simple. Give value first, earn attention, then sell. Let me give you 9 types of social media content you can mix into your strategy. Type number 1. Educational content. How-to tips, quick lessons, and industry insights. This provides value and establishes your expertise Type number two, behind the scenes content Show your process, your team, and daily operations This humanizes your brand Type number three, user-generated content Share customer photos, testimonials, and reviews This builds social proof and trust Type number four, interactive content polls, questions, quizzes, and challenges. This boosts engagement and helps you learn about your audience. Type number five, curated content. Share valuable content from others in your industry. This positions you as a helpful resource, not just self-promotional. Type number six, stories. Casual, authentic, day-in-the-life content. This creates personal connection. Type number seven, live content. Q&A sessions, tutorials, events, and interviews. This creates real-time engagement and urgency. Type number 8, entertainment. Memes, humor, and relatable content. This shows personality and increased shares. Type number 9, announcements. Product launches, updates, and news. This is your promotional content, your 20%. Mix these content types to keep your feed diverse and interesting. Variety keeps people engaged. One of the biggest mistakes people make is posting inconsistently. The solution is a content calendar. Here's a sample weekly calendar. On Monday, post motivational content to start the week on Instagram and LinkedIn. An example would be a Monday mindset post or success story. On Tuesday, share educational tips or how-tos on Facebook and Twitter. For example, a quick marketing automation tip. On Wednesday, post behind-the-scenes content on Instagram stories and LinkedIn. Show your team or process. On Thursday, share user-generated content or testimonials across all platforms. Feature a customer success story. On Friday, post fun or entertaining content on Instagram and TikTok. Think relatable memes or Friday feels. On Saturday, share curated content or industry news on LinkedIn and Facebook. Share a valuable article with your commentary. On Sunday, post inspirational or reflective content on Instagram. A weekly reflection or motivational quote. This is just a template. Adjust it based on your audience and capacity. The key principle is this. 3 to 5 posts per week is better than daily inconsistency. Your audience needs to see you regularly to remember you. Now let me teach you the formula for writing social media posts that actually get attention and engagement. Every post needs 3 elements. The hook, the value, and the call to action. Element number 1 is the hook. which is your first line. This is what stops the scroll. You need to grab attention immediately with a bold statement, a surprising fact, or a provocative question. For example, you are losing 70% of your leads because of this one mistake. That makes people stop and think, wait, what mistake? A bad example would be, here is a marketing tip. That is boring and generic. Nobody stops for that. Element number two is the value. Which is the body of your post? This is where you deliver on the promise of your hook. Teach something, solve a problem, share a story. For example, most businesses don't follow up within 5 minutes. Here is how to automate instant responses. A bad example would be, our product is great and has many features. That is promotional and provides no value. Element number three is the call to action. Tell people what to do next. Give one clear next step. For example, save this post. Comment automation if you want my free workflow template. A bad example would be, check out our website. That is too vague and generic. A few formatting tips. Keep post shorts and scannable. Break lines for readability. Use emojis strategically but not excessively And front-load important information because many people only read the first two lines. This formula works on every platform. Let's talk about hashtags, which help new people discover your content. Hashtags categorize your content and make it searchable. Think of them like putting your content in folders that people can browse. How many hashtags should you use? It depends on the platform. On Instagram, use 10-15 hashtags per post. You can put them in the caption or the first comment. Instagram loves hashtags and they significantly boost reach. On LinkedIn, use 3-5 hashtags and keep it professional. More than that, looks spammy. On Twitter or X, use 1-2 hashtags that are concise and relevant. Twitter is fast-paced and too many hashtags clutter your message. On TikTok, use 3-5 hashtags, mixing trending hashtags and niche hashtags. On Facebook, use 1-3 hashtags. Facebook users don't engage with many hashtags like Instagram users do. What types of hashtags should you mix? Use 1-2 broad hashtags like marketing tips or small business. Add 2-3 niche hashtags like email marketing automation or GHL tips. and use 1 branded hashtags with your brand name or campaign name Pro tip research hashtags before using them Check if they are active and relevant Some hashtags are banned or associated with spam so always verify Social media is social That means you need to engage actively, not just broadcast. There are two sides to engagement, responding and initiating. On the responding side, reply to every comment within 24 hours. Answer direct messages and messages promptly Acknowledge tags and mentions Thank people for shares and address questions publicly when it helps others On the initiating side, comment on posts in your niche every single day Share and tag relevant creators Join conversations in groups Engage with your target audience's content And ask questions to create discussions Here's the golden rule Social media is social, so conversation beats broadcasting. Give attention to get attention. And here is a bonus tip. The first hour after your post is critical for reach. The more engagements your post get in that first hour, the more the algorithm will push it to a wider audience. So be active right after posting. Reply to comments, engage with others, and boost your own post momentum. To stay consistent without burning out, use scheduling tools to batch your content creation. Buffer is simple and affordable. It has free version and paid plans starting at about $6 per month. It is great for beginners and small teams. Hootsuite is more comprehensive. It has a free version and paid plans starting at about $99 per month. It is excellent for agencies and larger teams with team collaboration features. Later is best for visual content creators, especially Instagram-focused. It has a free version and paid plans starting at about $18 per month with a visual drag-and-drop planner. Metricool combines scheduling with analytics and competitor tracking. It has free and paid versions. Native schedulers are also an option. Facebook, Instagram, and LinkedIn all have built-in scheduling tools that are completely free. Publer offers AI assistance and bulk scheduling with free and paid options. Here is my workflow recommendation. Schedule your content in batches. Set aside one day per week or one day per month to create and schedule multiple posts. This saves time and keeps you consistent. But stay active for real-time engagement. Scheduling is great for posting, but engagement should always be personal and timely. You cannot improve what you do not measure. So let's talk about which social media metrics actually matter. I want to show you this as a pyramid with three tiers. The top tier is vanity metrics. These include follower count, likes, and impressions. Track these but do not obsess over them. They show a reach but do not prove value to the business. The middle tier is engagement metrics. These are very important. Track engagement rate, which is likes plus comments plus shares divided by followers. Track actual comments to see quality conversations happening. Track shares to see if content resonates enough that people share it. And track saves to see if content is valuable enough to keep. The bottom tier is business metrics, and this is the most important. Track click-through rate to see how many people click to your website or landing page Track lead conversions to see how many became leads Track sales conversion to see how many became customers And track ROI to see revenue generated versus cost Focus on the bottom tier That is what clients care about Follower count is nice, but leads and sales are what matter Let me warn you about 7 common social media mistakes so you can avoid them. Mistake number 1. Posting inconsistently or disappearing for weeks. The algorithm punishes inconsistency and your audience forgets you. Fix. Post 3-5 times per week, minimum using scheduling tools. Mistake number 2. Only posting promotional content. This violates the 80-20 rule and people unfollow. Fix, follow 80% value, 20% promotion Mistake number three, buying followers or engagement Fake followers do not convert and it damages your credibility Fix, grow organically with a real engaged audience Mistake number four, ignoring comments and messages This kills relationships and shows you do not care Fix, respond within 24 hours and engage actively Mistake number five, using irrelevant or banned hashtags. This reduces your reach and can get you shadow banned. Fix, research your hashtags and only use relevant ones. Mistake number six, posting without strategy or goals. This wastes time with no measurable results. Fix, define goals, create a strategy, and track metrics. Mistake number seven, copying competitors exactly. This creates no differentiation and looks unoriginal. Fix, learn from competitors but find your unique voice. Avoid these mistakes and you are already ahead of 80% of brands on social media. Now let me give you eight proven strategies for growing your social media following organically. Strategy number one, post consistently. Aim for three to five times per week minimum. Consistency beats perfection every time. Strategy number two, optimize your profile. Make sure you have a clear bio, professional photo, linked in bio, and relevant keywords. Strategy number three, engage daily. Spend 30 minutes per day engaging with others in your niche. Comment, like, share, build relationships. Strategy number four, collaborate. Partner with others through guest posts, account takeovers, and shoutouts. Collaboration expands your reach. Strategy number five. Use hashtags strategically. Research and use relevant hashtags to increase discovery. Strategy number six. Run contests or giveaways. Incentivize follows, shares, and tags to create viral growth. Strategy number seven. Share valuable content. Solve problems, educate and entertain. Value attracts followers naturally. Strategy number eight. Be authentic. Show your personality, tell stories and be human, not corporate. People connect with people, not logos. Remember, growth is a byproduct of value and consistency. Focus on providing value and growth will follow naturally. Now, let me show you how social media integrates with marketing automation. This is where everything comes together. Integration flow number one. You post valuable content on social media with a link to a landing page. A visitor clicks that link and opts in for your lead magnet. They enter your CRM and an automated email nurture sequence begins. Social media is the top of your funnel and automation takes over from there Integration Flow No 2 Someone comments on your social media post You engage and build a relationship. You send them a direct message with a helpful resource. You invite them to join your email list or book a call. The relationship you build on social media leads to conversion through automation. Integration Flow No. 3. You run paid social ads that drive traffic to landing page. You track visitors with pixels and UTM codes. People who visit but do not convert get retargeted with more ads and automated email sequences. This creates multi-touch attribution. Key integration points to remember. Use UTM parameters to track which social traffic converts. Retarget social visitors with email sequences. Use social engagement to qualify leads in your CRM, and automate social proof collection from happy customers. Social media is the top of your funnel. It attracts attention and starts conversations. Marketing automation takes over from there to nurture, convert, and delight those needs. Let me share a real success story to show you what is possible. Before, imagine a marketing consultant with 350 followers on LinkedIn posting randomly with no strategy. Engagement is minimal, 5 to 10 likes per post. She's getting zero leads from LinkedIn. She has no strategy, no consistency, and no results. Strategy implemented. She decides to focus exclusively on LinkedIn. She commits to posting 3 times per week, Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. Her content mix is 80% educational posts about marketing automation and 20% client wins and offers. She spends 30 minutes every day engaging with her target audience, and she uses 3-5 relevant hashtags on every post. She commits to this for 6 months. After 6 months, she has grown to 5,200 followers. Her engagement has exploded to 50-150 likes and 10-30 comments per post. She is generating 15-20 qualified leads per month from LinkedIn. She has closed 4 clients directly from LinkedIn worth a total of P180,000. LinkedIn became her primary lead source. The key lesson, consistency plus value plus engagement equals result. Social media works when you commit to it as a real marketing channel, not just a site activity. Let's recap what you learned in part 4 about social media marketing. You learned that social media is the third pillar focused on building relationships where your audience already spends time. 4.9 billion people use social media so your audience is definitely there. Choose one to two platforms based on where your specific audience spends time, not just what is trendy. Follow the 80-20 rule. 80% value, 20% promotion. Mix nine content types including educational, behind the scenes, user-generated, interactive, curated, stories, live entertainment, and announcement. Engage actively by responding to all comments and initiating conversations daily. Use scheduling tools to batch content creation but stay active for real-time engagement. Track business metrics like leads and conversions, not just vanity metrics like follower count. And integrate social media with your automation systems, because social is the top of your funnel. Congratulations! You have completed Lesson 3.1. Let's do a complete recap of everything you learned. We covered the three pillars of inbound marketing. Pillar number one, content marketing. You create valuable content that attracts and educates your audience. This includes blog posts, videos, lead magnets, guides, and podcasts. The result is building trust and authority. Pillar number two. Pillar number two. SEO. Pillar number two. SEO. You optimize so people can find your content on Google. This includes keyword research, on-page optimization, and earning backlinks. The result is driving organic traffic. Pillar number three, social media. You build relationships where your audience spends time. This includes posts, engagement, community building, and conversation. The result is amplifying your reach and building community. All three pillars work together to attract customers naturally. Content gives you something valuable to share. SEO makes that content discoverable in search engines. Social media amplifies reach and builds relationships. The result? Customers find you, trust you, and buy from you. This is the power of inbound marketing. When you master these three pillars, you attract customers naturally without cold calling or pushy ads. Now let me connect this back to your role as a marketing automation specialist. How do you bring inbound marketing to life through automation? For content marketing, you build landing pages for lead magnets. You set up automated content delivery systems. You create email sequences that nurture leads with valuable content. And you automate blog post distribution across channels. For SEO, you ensure tracking is properly set up using Google Analytics and Google Search Console. You optimize landing pages and forms for target keywords. You can build automated internal linking systems. and you track keyword rankings and organic traffic performance. For social media, you schedule social content consistently using tools. You automate lead capture from social media traffic. You set up integration between social media and your CRM. And you track social conversions and return on investment. All three pillars feed into your marketing automation platform. You build the automation systems that make inbound marketing scalable. Without automation, inbound marketing requires massive manual effort. With automation, it runs efficiently and scales beautifully. This is why understanding inbound marketing is essential for your role. This completes Lesson 3.1. You now have a comprehensive understanding of inbound marketing and its three pillars, content, SEO, and social media. In Lesson 3.2, we are going to flip the coin and talk about outbound marketing. You will learn what outbound marketing is, including paid ads, cold email, and SMS campaigns. You will understand when outbound makes sense strategically. You will explore paid advertising platforms like Facebook ads, Google ads, and LinkedIn ads. You will learn how to create cold email and SMS campaigns that actually work. You will understand compliance requirements and best practices. And you will learn how to automate outbound campaigns. Here is the context. Inbound marketing attracts customers to you. Outbound marketing reaches out to customers. Both have their place, and the best marketing automation specialists know when and how to use each approach. Take a break, review your notes from this lesson, and I will see you in lesson 3.2 where we dive into outbound marketing. Great work completing this lesson. Thank you for watching!

visualDescriptions.description:
The image presents a comprehensive overview of social media marketing, providing a clear definition, key differences, and the social media promise. The main points are:

* **Definition**
	+ Social Media Marketing = Using social platforms to attract, engage, and build relationships with your target audience.
* **Key Difference**
	+ Not just broadcasting - it's about conversation and community.
* **The Social Media Promise**
	+ "If I show up consistently, provide value, and engage authentically, I will build a community of people who know, like, and trust me."
* **Why It's Called "Social"**
	+ A flowchart illustrating the process:
		- You share valuable content
		- Relationship Builds
		- Audience engages and responds

In summary, the image effectively communicates the essence of social media marketing, emphasizing its focus on building relationships and engaging with the target audience through consistent and authentic interactions. By providing valuable content, businesses can establish trust and foster a loyal community.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to social media metrics, categorized into three primary sections: Vanity Metrics, Engagement Metrics, and Business Metrics. The title, "Social Media Metrics That Matter," is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by the subtitle "What to track and why."

**Vanity Metrics**

* **Definition:** Track but don't obsess
* **Examples:**
	+ Follower Count
	+ Likes
	+ Impressions
* **Description:** Shows reach, but doesn't prove value

**Engagement Metrics**

* **Definition:** Very important
* **Examples:**
	+ Engagement Rate
	+ Comments
	+ Shares
	+ Saves
* **Description:** Shows if content resonates & builds relationships

**Business Metrics**

* **Definition:** Most important
* **Examples:**
	+ Click-Through Rate
	+ Lead Conversions
	+ Sales Conversions
	+ ROI
* **Description:** Proves social media drives business results

A key takeaway from the image is emphasized in a black bar at the bottom: "Focus on the bottom tier - that's what clients care about." This suggests that while vanity metrics and engagement metrics are important, business metrics are ultimately the most crucial for measuring the success of social media efforts.

The image features a clean and organized design, with a white background and a logo for "LIBRE" in the top-right corner. Overall, the image provides a clear and concise overview of the different types of social media metrics and their relative importance.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to common social media mistakes and their corresponding solutions, titled "7 Social Media Mistakes to Avoid." The infographic is divided into two columns, with the left column listing the mistakes and the right column providing the solutions.

**Mistakes:**

*   **Inconsistent Posting**
    *   Why: Algorithm punishes, audience forgets you
*   **Only Promotional Content**
    *   Why: Violates 80/20 rule, people unfollow
*   **Buying Followers**
    *   Why: Fake followers don't convert, damages credibility
*   **Ignoring Comments**
    *   Why: Kills relationships, shows you don't care
*   **Irrelevant Hashtags**
    *   Why: Reduces reach, can get shadowbanned
*   **No Strategy/Goals**
    *   Why: Wastes time, no measurable results
*   **Copying Competitors**
    *   Why: No differentiation, looks unoriginal

**Solutions:**

*   **Post Consistently**
    *   3-5 times per week minimum, use scheduling tools
*   **Follow 80/20 Rule**
    *   80% value-driven content, 20% promotion
*   **Grow Organically**
    *   Build a real, engaged audience over time
*   **Respond Promptly**
    *   Reply within 24 hours and engage actively
*   **Strategic Tagging**
    *   Research hashtags, use relevant ones only
*   **Data-Driven Approach**
    *   Define goals, create strategy, track metrics
*   **Find Your Voice**
    *   Learn from competitors but differentiate

**Key Takeaway:**

*   Avoid these common mistakes and you're ahead of 80% of brands.

The infographic effectively highlights the importance of avoiding common social media mistakes and provides actionable solutions to help individuals and businesses improve their online presence. By following these guidelines, users can create a strong social media strategy that drives engagement and growth.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to growing a social media following through eight proven strategies for sustainable audience building. The title, "How to Grow Your Social Media Following," is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by the subtitle "8 proven strategies for sustainable audience building." The infographic is divided into two columns, each containing four boxes that outline the strategies.

* **POST CONSISTENTLY**
	+ 3-5 times per week minimum
	+ Consistency beats perfection
* **OPTIMIZE YOUR PROFILE**
	+ Clear bio
	+ Professional photo
	+ Link in bio
	+ Relevant keywords
* **ENGAGE DAILY**
	+ Spend 30 minutes per day engaging with others in your niche
* **COLLABORATE**
	+ Partner with others through guest posts, takeovers, and shoutouts
* **USE HASHTAGS STRATEGICALLY**
	+ Research and use relevant hashtags to increase discovery
* **RUN CONTESTS OR GIVEAWAYS**
	+ Incentivize follows, shares, and tags to boost reach
* **SHARE VALUABLE CONTENT**
	+ Solve problems
	+ Educate
	+ Entertain
	+ Value attracts followers
* **BE AUTHENTIC**
	+ Show personality
	+ Tell stories
	+ Be human, not corporate

The infographic also features a logo in the top-right corner, which reads "LIBRE" in green text, accompanied by a small green leaf above the letter "I." A tagline at the bottom of the image states, "Growth is a byproduct of value and consistency."

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise guide to growing a social media following through eight actionable strategies, emphasizing the importance of consistency, engagement, and authenticity. By following these tips, individuals can build a sustainable audience and increase their online presence.

The image presents a case study on the success of a marketing consultant's social media strategy on LinkedIn, titled "Social Media Success Story" with the subtitle "Real example: Marketing consultant on LinkedIn." The infographic is divided into three sections: "BEFORE," "STRATEGY IMPLEMENTED," and "AFTER (6 MONTHS)," each providing specific metrics and statistics.

**BEFORE**

* 350 Followers
* Random Posting schedule
* 5-10 Likes per post
* 0 Leads from LinkedIn

**STRATEGY IMPLEMENTED**

* **Platform**: Exclusive focus on LinkedIn
* **Mix**: 80% Edu / 20% Wins
* **Hashtags**: 3-5 relevant per post
* **Frequency**: 3x/week (Mon, Wed, Fri)
* **Engage**: 30 mins' daily outreach
* **Duration**: Committed for 6 months

**AFTER (6 MONTHS)**

* 5,200 Followers
* 50-150 Likes per post
* 15-20 Qualified Leads/mo
* 180,000 Pesos Closed

The infographic highlights the challenges faced by the marketing consultant before implementing a strategy, including an inconsistent and weak presence, and the significant improvements achieved after six months, including a substantial increase in followers, likes, leads, and revenue. The key takeaway is that consistency, value, and engagement are essential for achieving results on social media, as stated in the black banner at the bottom: "Consistency + Value + Engagement = Results. Social media works when you commit."

The infographic effectively demonstrates the impact of a well-planned social media strategy on a marketing consultant's online presence and business outcomes.

The image presents a summary of Part 4 of a social media marketing course, titled "Social Media Marketing Recap." The main points are:

* **What You Learned in Part 4**
	+ Third Pillar: Social media builds relationships where your audience already is.
		- No statistics provided
	+ Platform Focus: Choose 1 to 2 platforms based on where your audience spends time.
		- No statistics provided
	+ Content Mix: Use 9 types including educational, behind-the-scenes, and interactive.
		- No statistics provided
	+ Scheduling: Use tools to batch content, but stay active for real-time engagement.
		- No statistics provided
	+ Automation: Social is the top of the funnel; integrate it with your CRM.
		- No statistics provided
* **Key Statistics and Takeaways**
	+ 4.9 Billion People: Your audience is definitely there; meet them where they scroll.
		- 4.9 billion people
	+ 80/20 Rule: Give value 80% of the time, promote only 20%.
		- 80% value, 20% promotion
	+ Active Engagement: Respond to all comments and initiate conversations daily.
		- No statistics provided
	+ Track Metrics: Focus on business metrics (leads/conversions), not just vanity stats.
		- No specific statistics provided

The image appears to be a recap of a lesson on social media marketing, highlighting key takeaways and statistics. The left side of the image outlines the main points learned in Part 4, while the right side provides a final wrap-up and a preview of upcoming lessons. The image does not provide a detailed analysis or interpretation of the information, but rather serves as a concise summary of the material covered. Overall, the image effectively conveys the importance of social media marketing and provides actionable tips for businesses looking to leverage this channel.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the role of a Marketing Automation Specialist, highlighting their responsibilities in bringing inbound marketing to life. The title, "Your Role as a Marketing Automation Specialist," sets the tone for the infographic, which is divided into three main sections: Content Marketing, SEO, and Social Media.

**Content Marketing**

*   Build landing pages for lead magnets
*   Set up automated content delivery
*   Create email sequences that nurture with content
*   Automate blog post distribution

**SEO**

*   Ensure tracking is set up (Google Analytics, Search Console)
*   Optimize landing pages and forms for keywords
*   Build automated internal linking
*   Track keyword rankings and organic traffic

**Social Media**

*   Schedule social content consistently
*   Automate lead capture from social traffic
*   Set up social media to CRM integration
*   Track social conversions and ROI

The infographic also highlights the key benefit of marketing automation: making inbound marketing scalable by connecting content, traffic, and engagement into a unified system. This allows businesses to streamline their marketing efforts, increase efficiency, and drive more conversions.

Overall, the image provides a clear and concise overview of the role of a Marketing Automation Specialist and the importance of marketing automation in bringing inbound marketing to life.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the five major social media platforms, providing valuable insights for businesses and individuals seeking to establish a strong online presence. The title, "The 5 Major Social Media Platforms," sets the stage for an in-depth analysis of each platform.

**Platform Overview**

*   **Facebook**
    *   **User Demographics:** 3B users, 35+ years old
    *   **Best For:** Community building, groups, paid ads, local businesses
    *   **Content Types:** Posts, Videos, Stories, Groups
*   **Instagram**
    *   **User Demographics:** 2B users, 18-34 years old
    *   **Best For:** Visual brands, lifestyle, influencer marketing
    *   **Content Types:** Photos, Reels, Stories, IGTV
*   **LinkedIn**
    *   **User Demographics:** 900M users, professionals
    *   **Best For:** B2B, thought leadership, networking, recruiting
    *   **Content Types:** Articles, Posts, Videos, Pages
*   **Twitter/X**
    *   **User Demographics:** 550M users, diverse
    *   **Best For:** Real-time updates, news, thought leadership, CS
    *   **Content Types:** Tweets, Threads, Spaces, DMs
*   **TikTok**
    *   **User Demographics:** 1B users, Gen Z
    *   **Best For:** Short video, viral content, creative storytelling
    *   **Content Types:** Shorts, Trends, Challenges, Live

**Key Takeaways**

*   **Strategic Selection:** Choose platforms based on where your audience is.
*   **Mastering Platforms:** Don't try to be on all platforms; master 1 to 2 where your audience actually is.

In summary, the image provides a concise and informative guide to the five major social media platforms, highlighting their unique characteristics, user demographics, and content types. By understanding these key factors, businesses and individuals can make informed decisions about which platforms to prioritize and how to effectively engage with their target audience.

The image presents a concise and informative guide to the 80/20 social media content rule, titled "The 80/20 Social Media Content Rule" in bold black text at the top. The subtitle, "What to post and when to sell," provides context for the rule.

**Visual Representation:**
A circular diagram on the left illustrates the 80/20 rule, with a green section representing 80% and a blue section representing 20%. The diagram is labeled "80/20 RULE."

**Content Breakdown:**
Two rectangular boxes to the right of the diagram outline the types of content that fall under each category:

**Value-Driven Content (80%):**

* Education/Tips
* Entertainment
* Inspiration
* Problem Solving
* Behind-the-Scenes
* User-Generated Content

**Promotional Content (20%):**

* Product Launches
* Sales Offers
* CTAs to Buy
* Limited-time Deals

**Key Takeaways:**

* "Give value 80% of the time to earn trust."
* "Ask for the sale 20% of the time."

**Additional Insights:**
A dark blue banner at the bottom features white text that reads, "'People don't follow you for ads - they follow for value.' Earn attention first, then sell."

**Branding:**
The top-right corner displays the logo for "LIBRE," accompanied by a small green leaf icon. The top-left corner features a green rectangle with the words "CONTENT STRATEGY" in black text. The background of the image is white.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to creating an effective social media content strategy, titled "9 Types of Social Media Content to Mix." The title is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by the logo for Libre in the upper right corner.

**Content Strategy Categories**

The infographic is divided into nine distinct categories, each represented by a unique icon and color scheme. These categories are:

*   **EDUCATIONAL**: How-to tips, quick lessons, and industry insights that solve problems.
    *   Provide value & establish expertise
*   **BEHIND-THE-SCENES**: Showcase your process, team, and daily operations unfiltered.
    *   Humanize your brand
*   **USER-GENERATED**: Reshare customer photos, testimonials, and honest reviews.
    *   Build social proof & trust
*   **INTERACTIVE**: Polls, questions, quizzes, and challenges that require action.
    *   Boost engagement & learn audience
*   **CURATED**: Share valuable content from other experts in your industry.
    *   Position as helpful resource
*   **STORIES**: Casual, authentic, day-in-the-life content that disappears.
    *   Create personal connection
*   **LIVE VIDEO**: Q&A sessions, tutorials, events, and interviews in real-time.
    *   Real-time engagement & urgency
*   **ENTERTAINMENT**: Memes, humor, and relatable content that lightens the mood.
    *   Show personality & increase shares
*   **ANNOUNCEMENTS**: Product launches, company updates, and big news (the 20%).
    *   Keep audience informed

**Key Takeaways**

The infographic provides a clear and concise overview of the various types of social media content that can be used to engage audiences and achieve specific goals. By incorporating a mix of these content types, businesses can create a well-rounded social media strategy that resonates with their target audience.

The image presents a concise guide to crafting effective social media posts, titled "The Social Media Post Formula" in bold black text at the top. The title is accompanied by the subtitle "3 elements every post needs to succeed" in smaller gray text.

**Header Section:**

* A green rectangle with the words "COPYWRITING MASTERCLASS" in white text
* The Libre logo, featuring a green leaf and the word "LIBRE" in green text, positioned in the top-right corner

**Main Content:**

The main content is divided into three sections, each representing a crucial element of a successful social media post:

1. **The Hook (Red Section)**
	* Purpose: Stop the scroll
	* How to write it: Start with a bold statement, surprising fact, or provocative question that creates immediate curiosity
	* Examples:
		+ Good: "You're losing 70% of your leads because of this one mistake."
		+ Bad: "Here's a marketing tip."
2. **The Value (Green Section)**
	* Purpose: Deliver on promise
	* How to write it: Teach something new, solve a specific problem, or share a relatable story that builds trust
	* Examples:
		+ Good: "Most businesses don't follow up within 5 mins. Here's how to automate instant responses..."
		+ Bad: "Our product is great and has many features."
3. **The CTA (Blue Section)**
	* Purpose: Next steps
	* How to write it: Give one clear, specific instruction on what to do next; don't be vague
	* Examples:
		+ Good: "Save this post. Comment 'automation' if you want my free workflow template."
		+ Bad: "Check out our website."

**Footer Section:**

* A row of six bullet points providing formatting tips:
	+ Formatting Tips
	+ Keep it short & scannable
	+ Break lines for readability
	+ Use emojis strategically
	+ Front-load important info

Overall, the image provides a clear and concise guide to creating effective social media posts, highlighting the importance of a hook, value, and a clear call-to-action.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to developing an effective hashtag strategy for social media platforms. The title, "Hashtag Strategy," is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by a brief introduction that explains the purpose of using hashtags.

**What Hashtags Do**

* Categorize your content and help new people discover you
* Think of them like putting your content in searchable folders

**How Many to Use Per Platform**

* **Instagram**: 10-15 (Caption or First Comment)
* **LinkedIn**: 3-5 (Keep it Professional)
* **TikTok**: 1-2 (Concise & Relevant)
* **TikTok Alternative**: 3-5 (Mix Trending & Niche)
* **Facebook**: 1-3 (Low Engagement Utility)

**The Strategic Mix**

* **Broad**: 1-2 (#MarketingTips #SmallBiz)
* **Niche**: 2-3 (#EmailAutomation #GHL Tips)
* **Branded**: 1-2 (#YourBrand #CampaignName)

**Pro Tip**

* Research hashtags before using - check if they're active, relevant, and not banned or spammy

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise guide to creating a successful hashtag strategy, including the optimal number of hashtags to use on each platform, the importance of a strategic mix of broad, niche, and branded hashtags, and a valuable tip for researching hashtags before use. By following these guidelines, individuals can effectively utilize hashtags to increase their online visibility and engagement.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to social media engagement best practices, titled "Social Media Engagement: The Two-Way Rule." The title is accompanied by the subtitle "Give attention to get attention."

**Key Takeaways:**

* **Respond:** React to Your Audience
	+ Reply to every comment within 24 hours
	+ Answer DMs and messages promptly
	+ Acknowledge tags and mentions
	+ Thank people for shares
	+ Address questions publicly when helpful
* **Initiate:** Start Conversations
	+ Comment on posts in your niche daily
	+ Share and tag relevant creators
	+ Join conversations in groups
	+ Engage with your target audience's content
	+ Ask questions and create discussions

**Additional Tips:**

* **Social Media is Social - Conversation Beats Broadcasting:** A reminder that social media is a two-way conversation, not a one-way broadcast.
* **Bonus Tip:** The first hour after posting is critical for reach. Engage actively right away.

**Visual Elements:**

* A green bar at the top with the text "ENGAGEMENT BEST PRACTICES"
* A logo in the top-right corner featuring a green plant growing out of the letter "I" and the word "LIBRE"
* A white background with a green stripe at the top

Overall, the image provides a clear and concise guide to social media engagement best practices, emphasizing the importance of responding to audience interactions and initiating conversations to build a strong online presence.
